skill.name: seedance-v2
skill.description: Generate cinematic short-form video with ByteDance Seedance 2.0 Pro on RunComfy. Documents Seedance 2.0 Pro's strengths (multi-modal references — up to 9 images, 3 videos, 3 audio — synchronized in-pass audio with natural lip-sync, cinematic motion refinement), the 4–15s duration schema, and when to route to HappyHorse 1.0 / Wan 2.7 / Kling instead. Calls `runcomfy run bytedance/seedance-v2/pro` through the local RunComfy CLI. Triggers on "seedance", "seedance 2", "seedance v2", "seedance pro", "bytedance video", or any explicit ask to generate video with this model.
skill.summary: AI-powered cinematic video generation with native lip-sync using ByteDance Seedance 2.0 Pro via RunComfy CLI
skill.author: agentspace-so

security_audit.summary: All static findings are false positives. The skill is a video generation documentation file with CLI command examples in markdown code blocks. The external_commands alerts flag markdown code fences (```bash) rather than actual shell execution. Hardcoded URLs are legitimate documentation links. Path traversal patterns are placeholder paths in examples. Token storage uses standard secure CLI practices with mode 0600 permissions. No malicious patterns confirmed.

high_findings.title: False Positive: Ruby/shell backtick pattern in markdown code blocks
high_findings.description: The static scanner flags backtick patterns in markdown code fences as 'Ruby/shell backtick execution'. These are documentation code examples showing CLI commands like `runcomfy run bytedance/seedance-v2/pro`. No shell execution occurs - these are markdown syntax for inline code and fenced code blocks. Verified at lines 24-26, 70-74, 78-87, 91-100, and throughout the document.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: Code fences (```bash) and inline backticks in markdown are documentation syntax, not shell backtick execution. The content describes CLI usage examples with hardcoded commands, not dynamic code generation.

high_findings.title: False Positive: Path traversal in documentation placeholders
high_findings.description: The static scanner flags `<absolute/path>` as path traversal. These are documentation placeholders showing users where to specify their output directory. They are not actual path traversal exploits - the skill documentation is teaching users how to use CLI arguments properly.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The placeholder <absolute/path> is standard documentation syntax teaching users CLI argument usage. No user input is processed into file paths without validation.

high_findings.title: False Positive: Weak cryptographic algorithm on 'seed' keyword
high_findings.description: The static scanner flags 'seed' as a weak cryptographic algorithm indicator. In this context, 'seed' refers to a video generation reproducibility parameter (line 64 in the schema table), not cryptography. This is a completely different semantic context.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The word 'seed' appears in a video generation context (schema field for reproducibility), not cryptographic usage. No actual cryptographic operations are performed in this documentation file.

medium_findings.title: Hardcoded URLs are legitimate documentation links
medium_findings.description: Static scanner flagged hardcoded URLs at lines 14, 20, 169, and 173. These are legitimate documentation links to runcomfy.com, docs.runcomfy.com, and model-api.runcomfy.net. No malicious URLs detected.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: All flagged URLs point to runcomfy.com and associated domains - the legitimate service provider for this skill. No suspicious external endpoints detected.

medium_findings.title: Token file storage at ~/.config/runcomfy/token.json
medium_findings.description: The skill documents storing API tokens in ~/.config/runcomfy/token.json. This is a standard CLI tool pattern and the documentation explicitly mentions using mode 0600 (owner-only) permissions. For CI environments, the skill recommends using RUNCOMFY_TOKEN environment variable instead.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: Standard secure CLI practice with documented permission requirements. Token storage is explicitly documented with security controls.

low_findings.title: False Positive: System reconnaissance patterns
low_findings.description: The static scanner flagged lines 42 and 112 as 'system reconnaissance'. Line 42 is a routing condition ('If the user said Seedance...') and line 112 documents reference media specs. These are not reconnaissance patterns - they are legitimate skill routing logic and documentation.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: No system information gathering detected. Line 42 is prompt routing logic, line 112 is documentation about reference media specifications.

content.user_title: Generate Cinematic Videos with AI Lip-Sync
content.value_statement: Creating professional spokesperson videos and brand-consistent short-form content requires combining multiple tools and manual lip-sync work. Seedance 2.0 Pro on RunComfy generates cinematic videos up to 15 seconds with native lip-synced audio in a single pass using multi-modal references.
actual_capabilities: Generate 4-15 second cinematic videos with text prompts up to 1000 words
Create native lip-synced audio in a single pass using the generate_audio parameter
Use up to 9 image references, 3 video clips, and 3 audio files as multi-modal inputs
Support multiple aspect ratios including 16:9, 9:16, 4:3, 3:4, 1:1, and 21:9
Reproduce results with seed control for variant testing
Download generated videos directly to a specified output directory via CLI
limitations: Maximum duration is 15 seconds per clip on this endpoint
Reference videos must be 2-15 seconds; audio files must be under 15MB and 2-15 seconds
Maximum resolution is 720p on the playground variant
Lip-sync quality depends on prompt clarity and may not be perfect under all conditions

use_cases.title: AI Spokesperson Videos
use_cases.description: Generate professional spokesperson content with natural lip-sync. Upload a character image reference and write the dialogue. The model creates consistent identity across frames with synchronized audio.

use_cases.title: Brand-Consistent Multi-Language Ads
use_cases.description: Create localized video ads by keeping the image reference constant and changing only the prompt text. The brand identity stays consistent while you produce multiple language variants.

use_cases.title: Cinematic Short-Form Previsualization
use_cases.description: Generate film previs with camera shot grammar instructions like 'slow push-in', 'locked-off wide', or 'handheld follow'. Combine image and video references for scene continuity testing.

prompt_templates.title: Simple Video Generation
prompt_templates.prompt: Generate a 5-second video of [describe scene, action, and mood]
prompt_templates.scenario: Basic text-to-video generation with default settings

prompt_templates.title: Lip-Synced Character Video
prompt_templates.prompt: Medium close-up of the person from the reference image, [describe dialogue tone], [describe camera movement], [describe lighting]
prompt_templates.scenario: Create spokesperson content using an uploaded character image

prompt_templates.title: Multi-Modal Cinematic Scene
prompt_templates.prompt: Subject from image 1 [describe action based on video 1], voice tone matches audio 1, [describe additional scene elements and camera work]
prompt_templates.scenario: Combine multiple reference materials for complex scenes

prompt_templates.title: Brand Consistency Test
prompt_templates.prompt: Same logo and brand style as reference image, [describe the brand story or message], [describe visual treatment and pacing]
prompt_templates.scenario: Test brand consistency across multiple video variants

output_examples.input: Generate a spokesperson video with a woman explaining today's special in a warm friendly tone
output_examples.output: A 5-second vertical video showing a woman in a cafe setting, speaking naturally with lip-sync matching the friendly explanatory tone, soft window lighting, and gentle ambient cafe sounds

output_examples.input: Create a cinematic wide shot of a character walking through a rain-soaked city street
output_examples.output: A 8-second 16:9 video with a moody urban atmosphere, character maintaining consistent identity from the reference image, realistic rain effects, and atmospheric lighting

output_examples.input: Generate product reveal with brand logo from reference, elegant slow-motion, premium feel
output_examples.output: A 6-second video showcasing the product with the brand identity holding consistent, cinematic slow-motion reveal, premium lighting, and professional presentation

best_practices: Use image references for stable identity elements like faces, costumes, and logos rather than describing them in text
Write camera and motion instructions in plain language like 'slow push-in' or 'locked-off wide' for predictable results
Match reference media aspect ratios to your target output to avoid unwanted crops or letterboxing
anti_patterns: Avoid mixing radically different aesthetic references like watercolor and photorealistic styles in the same request
Do not describe stable identity elements verbally; use image_url references instead for better consistency
Do not request videos longer than 15 seconds; segment longer content into multiple calls to avoid 422 errors

faq.question: What is the difference between Seedance 2.0 Pro and HappyHorse 1.0?
faq.answer: Seedance 2.0 Pro excels at lip-synced spokesperson content with multi-modal references. HappyHorse 1.0 currently ranks higher in blind-vote video quality for general content. Choose Seedance for dialogue-focused content, HappyHorse for raw video quality.

faq.question: How do I get the best lip-sync results?
faq.answer: Write clear dialogue descriptions with tone indicators like 'warm friendly conversational' or 'crisp newsroom delivery'. Use image_url for character identity. Keep prompts focused on the speaking portion. Audio quality of reference tracks also affects lip-sync accuracy.

faq.question: Can I generate videos longer than 15 seconds?
faq.answer: No, the endpoint maximum is 15 seconds. For longer content, generate multiple segments and plan your shots as a sequence. Use consistent image references across segments for continuity.

faq.question: What happens to external image and video URLs I provide?
faq.answer: The RunComfy model server fetches your reference media, not your local machine. Only submit URLs from sources you trust. Image-based prompt injection is a known risk for AI media generation models.

faq.question: How do I use this skill with Claude Code?
faq.answer: Once installed via npx skills add, the skill activates when you mention Seedance, Seedance 2, Seedance Pro, or ByteDance video. Claude Code will use the documented runcomfy CLI commands with your prompts.

faq.question: What exit codes should I handle in automation?
faq.answer: Exit 0 means success. Exit 64 is bad CLI arguments, exit 65 is bad input JSON, exit 69 is upstream server error, exit 75 is retryable (timeout or rate limit), exit 77 means authentication failed. Check docs.runcomfy.com/cli/troubleshooting for details.
